schools.summary: Gill weighs the Jewish reckonings of Samuel's age — fifty-two, sixty, or with Abarbinel above seventy — his labours from childhood having hastened old age.
Henry: two sad but not strange things — a good man growing old ('old age has cut his hair') and a good man's children turning aside; Samuel acted without nepotism.
Clarke and Wesley limit the sons' office: deputies and lieutenants only, never judges in the proper sense — Samuel remained the last judge to the day of his death.
Keil and Delitzsch place the verse at the head of the monarchy's establishment through Samuel's mediation; Ellicott reconstructs his life in periods and notes reviving Philistine power.

debates.question: How old was Samuel when he made his sons judges?

sides.position: about fifty-two, per the common Jewish notion, his labours having aged him early

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the names — Joel, 'Jehovah is God,' and Abiah, 'Jehovah a Father' — as tokens of Samuel's piety soon forgotten by his sons; Keil and Delitzsch confirm them as genuine Hebrew names.
Gill harmonises Joel with the Vashni of 1 Chronicles 6:28 and denies any identification with Joel the prophet.
Wesley locates Beer-sheba on Canaan's southern border, far from Ramah, Samuel still judging in the north.
Henry: settled at a distance from their father, the sons discovered themselves — honours change men's minds, and too often for the worse.

Clarke anatomises the three-word indictment — avarice, bribes that blind the eyes, judgment sold to the highest bidder; Wesley notes that opportunity and temptation exposed a corruption hidden from their father.
Gill: 'walked not in his ways' means not the circuit but the fear of God — they neither served God nor did justice to men as Samuel had.
Ellicott: bribery was expressly denounced in the Law; the ills that ruined Eli's house threatened Samuel's, but Samuel's sons were deposed and his line later restored in Heman.
Henry: the love of money is the root of all evil, pernicious especially in judges — grace is not hereditary, so none should be secure of themselves or theirs.
Keil and Delitzsch find in the request an unmistakable allusion to the law of the king in Deuteronomy 17:14; Ellicott identifies the elders as Israel's ancient representative assembly.
Gill: the elders were the heads of tribes and fathers of houses, the principal persons of age and authority.
Henry: a matter perfectly new and surprising, yet presented respectfully and by common consent, not in riot — the revolution came through the front door.
Gill: Samuel's age was no reproach and no disqualification — experience made him fitter, though he could not ride his circuits.
Henry: ungrateful and unjust to cast off an old servant who had spent his days doing them good; proof of bribery would have sufficed to remove the sons, but the elders had another project.
Wesley: their desires exceeded their reasons, and a king's corrupt sons would be harder to remove; Clarke: the request surrendered the theocracy for a general in battle.
Ellicott marks the elders' remarkable confidence in Samuel's disinterestedness; Keil and Delitzsch hear the petition framed almost in the words of Deuteronomy 17:14.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: the displeasure was not at the slight to Samuel, and the request itself was provided for in Deuteronomy 17 — the fault lay elsewhere than in the thing asked.
Clarke calls the demand a formal renunciation of the Divine government; Wesley traces it to distrust, ambition, and an itch after changes, dishonouring God.
Gill: Samuel was not displeased at being called old or at the mention of his sons, but at 'Give us a king to judge us.'
Henry: it cut him to the heart and drove him to his knees — they would anticipate God's counsel, who designed them a king in due time.

debates.question: Was the request for a king itself sinful, or lawful in substance though faithless in spirit and season?

sides.position: lawful in itself — Deuteronomy 17:14–20 had foreseen and provided for a king; Samuel's grief concerned the motive, not the institution
a sinful renunciation of the theocracy, sprung from distrust, ambition, and impatience of God's timing

schools.summary: Clarke: God grants in his displeasure what he withholds in his mercy; Wesley: the grant was in anger and for punishment, the contumely falling chiefly on God.
Henry: 'I share with thee in the affront' — Israel was weary of a theocracy under which they could not transgress at so cheap a rate as other nations.
Ellicott: a corner of the veil is lifted — the tender consolation of Samuel joined to God's sorrowful abandonment of the grand ideal, man's freedom marring the Creator's design.
Gill notes that God answered by vision or audible voice, and that the rejection was of God's own kingship.
Gill: the demand was no new thing but one continued series of ingratitude and rebellion against God and his servants since the Exodus.
Wesley: the words are for Samuel's comfort and vindication — 'Thou farest no worse than myself.'
Henry: Samuel must not marvel — they do as they always have done, transgressors from the womb; affecting new gods makes affecting new governors seem little.
Keil and Delitzsch: the Hebrew means to bear witness against them — Samuel enters their wrong upon the record, not merely warning or expounding.
Wesley: the protest aims, if possible, still to prevent their sin and misery; Clarke: mishpat is the despotic practice of the nations' kings, no charter of divine right — against Grotius.
Henry: God sometimes gratifies us in wrath as he crosses us in love; the grant lets them be beaten with their own rod and prevents worse evils.
Gill: hearken and appoint them a king as they desire — but only after the solemn testimony is delivered.
Keil and Delitzsch: Samuel reported all that God had said in vv.7–9 and then proclaimed the right of the king.
Gill: the whole message — rejection, concession, and warning — was laid before the people without suppression.
Henry: the law of God is our rule, the manner of men the measure of our expectations; be thankful and patient under government, and pray for rulers.

themes: telling all the words of the Lord
law of God versus manner of men

schools.summary: Gill: the 'manner' is what kings will do, not what they ought — with Aristotle, he that would have a man reign 'adds also a lust.'
Keil and Delitzsch defend the Hebrew: the state-carriage and saddle-horses of courtly display, not war-chariots; Ellicott: Samuel quietly asks whether Israel will trade its republic for a despotism.
Wesley on 'he will take': injuriously and by violence.
Henry: a multitude of attendants is the imaginary glory of great men, and it must be fed with other men's children.
Keil and Delitzsch: greatest and smallest commissions named to comprehend all ranks, still governed by 'he will take'; Ellicott: 'to ear' is old English for to plough.
Gill: posts of honour are no boon when they are not matter of choice and hang on the arbitrary will of a prince.
Wesley: appointments made for the king's own fancy or glory, honours attended with snares that their faint shadows of glory cannot recompense.
Henry: those who worked for their parents and themselves must work for him — and count it their preferment too.
Gill: confectionaries are workers in spices — or, with some versions, unguentariae, makers of ointments — a woman's trade in those times and places.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch render the word 'perfumers,' preparers of ointments for a court inordinately fond of scents.
Wesley: taking daughters is more grievous to parents and more dangerous to the daughters, because of the tenderness of their sex.
Henry: daughters hoped for houses and tables of their own must, willing or no, serve another man's luxury.
Gill: the verse means the produce of fields and vineyards, not the freeholds — else Ahab could have taken Naboth's vineyard at once, and Jezebel's plot were needless.
Wesley: the possessions themselves, taken by fraud or force as Ahab took Naboth's, and bestowed on the king's servants.
Keil and Delitzsch: the best fields and vineyards taken away and given to courtiers, the sarisim being chamberlains; the tithe of produce is a separate exaction.
Henry: the sting is in the giving — inheritances bestowed on servants who will become your masters over the ground you laboured.

debates.question: Does 'he will take your fields' mean the produce of the land or the estates themselves?

sides.position: the increase and fruits only — kings had no acknowledged power to seize freeholds, as the Naboth story shows
the possessions themselves, taken by fraud or force and given to royal servants

themes: favourites endowed at the people's cost

schools.summary: Gill: the tenth taken when the seed is grown and ripe — the exaction stands at the harvest itself.
Keil and Delitzsch: a fearful picture of tyranny drawn from the heathen kings, not written back from Israel's later experience.
Wesley: a second tenth imposed beside God's; and 'officers' is literally 'his eunuchs,' hinting at a court ordered against God's law.
Henry: those who grudge the church's tenths will pay a royal tenth levied with more rigour — consider whether the magnificence will quit cost.
Gill: the king drafts servants into his own family if he wants them or likes them better than his own.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read 'your best oxen' with the Septuagint — a one-letter copyist's error — since young men are already included among the sons and oxen naturally precede asses.
Wesley: taken by constraint, and without sufficient recompense.

debates.question: Should 'your goodliest young men' be read, or 'your best oxen' with the Septuagint?

sides.position: 'your best oxen' — an ancient one-letter copyist's error, restoring the natural pairing of servants with beasts of burden
the received text stands: the goodliest young men are one more class of the taken

schools.summary: Gill, with Kimchi: the tenth reaches herds as well as flocks — the levy is total.

Ellicott: 'ye shall be his servants' sums all that precedes — the loss of all political and social freedom, vindicated by the cry of 1 Kings 12:4; the passage bears internal evidence of pre-monarchic composition.
Wesley: he will use you like slaves and deprive you of the liberty you now enjoy.
Gill: the cry under an intolerable yoke is aggravated because self-inflicted — the chosen king cannot be shaken off.
Wesley: God will not hear in that day because they will not hear his counsel in this day.
Henry: distress brought on by our own irregular desires justly forfeits the comfort of prayer — 'your yoke is heavy, and I will add to it.'
Ellicott: the northern kings, Omri and Ahab foremost, fulfilled the seer's forebodings until Israel was carried captive out of the land.
Keil and Delitzsch: a warning alarming enough to compel desistance was simply repeated back; Ellicott: the refusal came from a formal assembly after debate, a deliberate vote for the yoke.
Clarke: they would have the king, his manner and all, notwithstanding the solemn warning.
Gill: what they refused was the counsel to be content with the government they had.
Henry: inordinate desires rob men of their reason — ten or twelve years' patience would have brought them David instead of Saul.

themes: inability to wait for God's time
Saul chosen where David was prepared

schools.summary: Gill: a king even at the price of slavery — such stupidity, when Israel's honour was precisely not to be like the nations.
Wesley: 'What stupidity!' — Israel's happiness was to dwell alone; Clarke parses the demand as judge, head, and general — every office of God given to a man.
Ellicott: a strangely painful haste to descend from the pedestal God had set them on; Keil and Delitzsch record the repeated demand with its reasons.
Henry: could they desire a battle better fought than by Samuel's prayer and God's thunder? Their first king fell in battle, as no judge ever did.
Gill: Samuel heard patiently and attentively, answered nothing, and reported all to the Lord.
Clarke: he laid the whole business before God at the altar in secret devotion; Wesley: repeated privately, for vindication, comfort, and as a foundation for prayer.
Ellicott: from the council chamber to the sanctuary in his 'Ramah of the Watchers,' pouring out his heart before his God-Friend.
Henry: God knows the case but will hear it from us — a holy familiarity, speaking in the ears of the Lord as one friend whispers with another.

Ellicott: the third repetition of 'hearken unto their voice' — God's perfect trust in a servant arranging his own supersession; Keil and Delitzsch note the elders sent home until the king should be shown.
Clarke: the people, not God, changed the government; the king was only the Lord's vicegerent, and absolute monarchy is always an evil; Wesley: dismissed to their occasions till they hear more.
Henry: 'so he gave them up to their own hearts' lusts' — yet the designation of the person must be left to God.
Gill: the Lord answered by audible voice or inward impulse, and the request was granted.

outliers: Clarke digresses from the text into a panegyric on the mixed British constitution as the providential mean between despotism and anarchy.
themes: toward David and David's greater Son
